jokes.text: Well, it was-- It was gross, all right.
jokes.setup: Vince mentions Jurassic Park was one of the highest grossing films in history
jokes.punchline: Hank deliberately misinterprets 'grossing' as 'gross' meaning disgusting
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank sidekick attempt at wit that shows his character perfectly - trying to be clever but slightly off-target. The wordplay is obvious but works because it's so Hank.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, it was-- It was gross, all right.

jokes.text: When they called you to the places. Did they say, uh, 'Mr. Vaughn, and the rest of you dinosaurs.'
jokes.setup: Continuing the Jurassic Park discussion
jokes.punchline: Larry awkwardly tries to make a dinosaur joke that doesn't quite land
jokes.explanation: Larry trying too hard to be funny and failing - perfect character moment showing his insecurity as a host.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: When they called you to the places. Did they say, uh, 'Mr. Vaughn, and the rest of you dinosaurs.'

jokes.text: Hank doesn't even have a fire, let alone irons.
jokes.setup: Hank mentioned having 'irons in the fire' earlier
jokes.punchline: Larry cuts deeper, saying Hank doesn't even have a fire
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the metaphor with perfect economy. Shows Larry's cruelty masked as humor - great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hank doesn't even have a fire, let alone irons.

jokes.text: Are you for real? Because you are. And next month, there's gonna be another flavor. Jerk.
jokes.setup: Larry was playing around during commercial
jokes.punchline: Vince takes it seriously and delivers a harsh truth about Larry's career prospects
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable truth delivered with venom. The 'flavor of the month' metaphor is cutting and the casual 'jerk' makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: Are you for real? Because you are. And next month, there's gonna be another flavor. Jerk.

jokes.text: don't let that toothless Ukrainian guy near it. Only give the keys to caro-- to caro!
jokes.setup: Hank giving detailed car care instructions
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and slightly racist concern about the Ukrainian worker
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character moment - obsessive about his car and casually inappropriate. The repetition of 'caro' shows his anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: don't let that toothless Ukrainian guy near it. Only give the keys to caro-- to caro!

jokes.text: Oh, who knows how long these gay cruises last. They're in international waters. They can do anything.
jokes.setup: Question about when Brian gets back
jokes.punchline: Hank's matter-of-fact delivery about gay cruises in international waters
jokes.explanation: Hank's casual homophobia mixed with his weird understanding of maritime law creates an absurd but character-perfect moment.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, who knows how long these gay cruises last. They're in international waters. They can do anything.

jokes.text: I just hit Hank's car.
jokes.setup: Beverly appears distressed
jokes.punchline: The simple, direct confession that sets up the episode's main plot
jokes.explanation: Simple setup joke that launches the main plot. Economy is excellent but it's more functional than funny.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I just hit Hank's car.

jokes.text: You're not parked anywhere near Hank's car. You're in another lot.
jokes.setup: Beverly hit Hank's car
jokes.punchline: She had to go out of her way to hit it - she's not even in the same lot
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that makes Beverly's bad driving even more absurd. The logistics make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Mary Lou
dialogue.line: You're not parked anywhere near Hank's car. You're in another lot.

jokes.text: I know. I'm such a bad driver.
jokes.setup: Beverly had to cross parking lots to hit Hank's car
jokes.punchline: Her casual acceptance of being a terrible driver
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan understatement. Beverly's matter-of-fact acceptance of her dangerous incompetence is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I know. I'm such a bad driver.

jokes.text: It was really big and foreign, kind of like a big boat.
jokes.setup: Asked which car she hit
jokes.punchline: Beverly's vague, unhelpful description
jokes.explanation: Beverly's unhelpful description shows her obliviousness. The 'big boat' description is endearingly useless.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: It was really big and foreign, kind of like a big boat.

jokes.text: You hit his bentley. Is that bad?
jokes.setup: Beverly's innocent question after learning she hit a Bentley
jokes.punchline: Her complete obliviousness to the severity
jokes.explanation: Beverly's innocent question perfectly captures her obliviousness. The pause before 'Is that bad?' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Mary Lou
dialogue.line: You hit his bentley.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Is that bad?

jokes.text: I thought I smelled hype in the air.
jokes.setup: Artie enters Norman's office
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical greeting referencing Norman's PR work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie line - cynical, industry-savvy, and delivered with his characteristic disdain for Hollywood BS.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I thought I smelled hype in the air.

jokes.text: Not for the prince of P.R.
jokes.setup: Artie asks if the cologne is too heavy
jokes.punchline: Norman's self-aware, slightly self-deprecating response
jokes.explanation: Norman's self-aware acknowledgment of his role. Good character moment but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: Not for the prince of P.R.

jokes.text: Larry Sanders poolside. You know, 'Legend in repose.' Sunlight makes the legend squint.
jokes.setup: Norman pitching interview concepts
jokes.punchline: The pretentious 'legend in repose' followed by the mundane squinting detail
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of PR speak. The grandiose 'legend in repose' undercut by the practical concern about squinting is brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: Larry Sanders poolside. You know, 'Legend in repose.' Sunlight makes the legend squint.

jokes.text: How 'bout in the living room on the couch? You know, 'Larry in the living room on the couch.'
jokes.setup: Norman's increasingly elaborate interview concepts
jokes.punchline: Artie's deliberately mundane, literal description
jokes.explanation: Artie's dry deflation of Norman's pretentious concepts is perfect. The literal repetition makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: How 'bout in the living room on the couch? You know, 'Larry in the living room on the couch.'

jokes.text: We go way back. Please...Don't ask. Please don't tell.
jokes.setup: Norman mentions knowing Maureen O'Boyle
jokes.punchline: His nervous reference to 'Don't Ask, Don't Tell' about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay but feels a bit forced. The military policy reference doesn't quite fit the context perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: We go way back. Please...Don't ask. Please don't tell.

jokes.text: We were lovers in '88 and again in '92.
jokes.setup: Asked about his relationship with Maureen
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific timeline of their affair
jokes.explanation: The specific years suggest a pattern of reunion affairs. Norman's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: We were lovers in '88 and again in '92.

jokes.text: It was hot-- Heh heh-- very hot.
jokes.setup: Asked what the affair was like
jokes.punchline: Norman's awkward, overly personal response with nervous laughter
jokes.explanation: Norman's inappropriate oversharing with nervous giggle is perfectly in character. The repetition emphasizes the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Norman
dialogue.line: It was hot-- Heh heh-- very hot.

jokes.text: I can't go to the habitat for fucking Humanity banquet in a dented bentley because some shitbag smashed into my fucking car!
jokes.setup: Hank discovering his car is damaged
jokes.punchline: His outrage focused on the charity event appearance rather than the damage itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his priorities are all about appearances and status. The 'fucking Humanity' malaprop is brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I can't go to the habitat for fucking Humanity banquet in a dented bentley because some shitbag smashed into my fucking car!

jokes.text: It was Vince fucking vaughn.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to figure out who hit his car
jokes.punchline: His immediate, confident accusation of the guest
jokes.explanation: Hank's paranoid leap to blaming the guest shows his character perfectly. The certainty makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It was Vince fucking vaughn.

jokes.text: my guess would be west hollywood. [Laughing] Unlisted. Like anyone gives a shit.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to get Vince Vaughn's address
jokes.punchline: His dismissive assessment of Vaughn's importance while actively seeking his address
jokes.explanation: Hank's contradictory behavior - needing Vince's address while dismissing his relevance - is perfectly hypocritical and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: my guess would be west hollywood. [Laughing] Unlisted. Like anyone gives a shit.

jokes.text: I get into a lot of accidents. Like, I don't know why. I just do. I have 5 or 6 a year. Or 7. Sometimes I get in-- It was 7 this year.
jokes.setup: Beverly apologizing for the accident
jokes.punchline: Her casual revelation of being a serial car crasher, with the number climbing as she talks
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation as Beverly reveals the true scope of her driving problems. The self-correcting numbers make it progressively funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I get into a lot of accidents. Like, I don't know why. I just do. I have 5 or 6 a year. Or 7. Sometimes I get in-- It was 7 this year.

jokes.text: You get in 5 or 6 accidents a year, and your car is a... It's a jetta. I'm-- I'm sorry. What? It's an '85 jetta.
jokes.setup: Beverly's accident rate revelation
jokes.punchline: The class contrast between her beat-up Jetta and his Bentley
jokes.explanation: Perfect class comedy - Hank's disbelief that someone in an old Jetta could damage his precious Bentley. The repeated 'what?' shows his shock.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You get in 5 or 6 accidents a year, and your car is a... It's a jetta. I'm-- I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's an '85 jetta.

jokes.text: And you are asking me to equate that with a $200,000 handcrafted bentley?
jokes.setup: The Jetta vs Bentley comparison
jokes.punchline: Hank's outraged, specific pricing of his car
jokes.explanation: Hank's materialistic outrage is perfect. The specific dollar amount and 'handcrafted' detail show his obsession with status.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And you are asking me to equate that with a $200,000 handcrafted bentley?

jokes.text: Is how great I thought you were on the 'shabad' telethon this year. Oh. Thanks. It's 'chabad.'
jokes.setup: Beverly trying to deflect with a compliment
jokes.punchline: Her mispronunciation and Hank's correction
jokes.explanation: Beverly's mispronunciation during her desperate deflection attempt is perfectly awkward. Hank's patient correction shows his ego needs the validation.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Is how great I thought you were on the 'shabad' telethon this year.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh. Thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: It's 'chabad.'

jokes.text: No one really knows how hard chabad is. I mean, 10 minutes of dancing that hora... That's a fucking marathon.
jokes.setup: Beverly complimenting Hank's telethon appearance
jokes.punchline: Hank's exaggerated assessment of the physical challenge
jokes.explanation: Hank's dramatic overstatement of the hora's difficulty is perfectly in character. The 'fucking marathon' comparison is absurd and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No one really knows how hard chabad is. I mean, 10 minutes of dancing that hora... That's a fucking marathon.

jokes.text: I'm actually wearing a #40 Emotional block right now.
jokes.setup: Discussion about retirement and not wearing makeup
jokes.punchline: Larry's specific makeup shade name that perfectly describes his emotional state
jokes.explanation: Brilliant joke that works on multiple levels - makeup humor, self-awareness, and perfect character insight. The specific shade number makes it feel real.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm actually wearing a #40 Emotional block right now.

jokes.text: It's your reflection in my eyes.
jokes.setup: Maureen compliments Arthur's appearance
jokes.punchline: Arthur's smooth, rehearsed comeback
jokes.explanation: Classic Arthur charm offensive. The line is cheesy but delivered with such confidence it works as character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: It's your reflection in my eyes.

jokes.text: This house was actually in architectural digest, and then I think it went to its head.
jokes.setup: Maureen comments on Larry's house being big
jokes.punchline: Larry personifying the house as having gotten conceited
jokes.explanation: Clever personification that shows Larry's wit. The idea of a house getting an ego from magazine coverage is both absurd and believable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This house was actually in architectural digest, and then I think it went to its head.

jokes.text: It was designed mostly for interviews like this, so that's great.
jokes.setup: Discussing his house
jokes.punchline: Larry's self-aware comment about his life being built around media appearances
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about celebrity life. Larry acknowledging his entire existence revolves around public appearances.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It was designed mostly for interviews like this, so that's great.

jokes.text: I cried. Now I'm fucked. It was a manly misting.
jokes.setup: Larry worrying about crying during the interview
jokes.punchline: Arthur's attempt to minimize it as 'manly misting'
jokes.explanation: Arthur's 'manly misting' is a perfect euphemism. The contrast between Larry's panic and Arthur's damage control is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I cried. Now I'm fucked.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: It was a manly misting.

jokes.text: Why not just televise my shrink sessions?
jokes.setup: Larry's embarrassment about crying on TV
jokes.punchline: His hyperbolic comparison to therapy sessions
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Larry's paranoia about public vulnerability. The therapy comparison is apt for a celebrity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Why not just televise my shrink sessions?

jokes.text: They never edit out the crying. That's the come shot.
jokes.setup: Discussion about editing Larry's emotional moment
jokes.punchline: Arthur's crude but accurate analogy about television
jokes.explanation: Arthur's cynical porn analogy for television's exploitation of emotional moments is both crude and insightful. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: They never edit out the crying. That's the come shot.

jokes.text: All dinners are business dinners.
jokes.setup: Distinction between business and personal dinner with Maureen
jokes.punchline: Arthur's cynical worldview about Hollywood relationships
jokes.explanation: Perfect Arthur wisdom - cynical but probably true in Hollywood. The absoluteness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: All dinners are business dinners.

jokes.text: Well, maybe i'll leave the knife out for you.
jokes.setup: Hank asking for a plate of food
jokes.punchline: Beverly's passive-aggressive threat
jokes.explanation: Beverly's dark humor about giving Hank a weapon shows her frustration. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, maybe i'll leave the knife out for you.

jokes.text: You got a crush on me.
jokes.setup: Beverly trying to confess about the car accident
jokes.punchline: Hank's completely wrong interpretation of her nervousness
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - Beverly's trying to confess and Hank completely misreads the situation. Shows his ego and obliviousness perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You got a crush on me.

jokes.text: Are you telling me that I have a bottle of champagne waiting at home for nothing?
jokes.setup: Maureen refusing to cut the crying scene
jokes.punchline: Arthur's reveal of his presumptuous preparation
jokes.explanation: Arthur's confidence that his seduction would work, revealed through the champagne detail, is perfectly arrogant and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: Are you telling me that I have a bottle of champagne waiting at home for nothing?

jokes.text: Find some dog shit, take it over to the fox lot, and smear it on Vince vaughn's car.
jokes.setup: Hank asking Beverly for a special favor
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous revenge scheme
jokes.explanation: Hank's elaborate, childish revenge plan is absurd and perfectly in character. The specific details make it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Find some dog shit, take it over to the fox lot, and smear it on Vince vaughn's car.

jokes.text: Hank, have you lost your mind? Is that a yes?

jokes.punchline: Hank's hopeful interpretation of her reaction
jokes.explanation: Hank's oblivious optimism in the face of Beverly's horror is perfectly delusional. His misreading of social cues is spot-on.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Hank, have you lost your mind?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Is that a yes?

jokes.text: Black-bottom pool for hollywood showbiz parties. Movie star like yourself.

jokes.punchline: The agent's Hollywood-specific sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Larry's impression captures the agent's Hollywood pandering. The 'black-bottom pool' detail is specific and funny.

dialogue.line: Black-bottom pool for hollywood showbiz parties. Movie star like yourself.

jokes.punchline: The agent's sudden honesty about the property's problems

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: fuck that. It's like a spider convention out there.

jokes.text: It's weird coming from saturday night live 'cause, uh, on the sitcom now I'm the only backstabber in the room.

jokes.punchline: Larry's self-aware observation about workplace dynamics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about television workplace culture. Larry's self-awareness about being the problem is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's weird coming from saturday night live 'cause, uh, on the sitcom now I'm the only backstabber in the room.

jokes.text: You stay away from her, shorty.
jokes.setup: Arthur warning Larry about Maureen
jokes.punchline: The casual 'shorty' insult
jokes.explanation: Arthur's casual height insult while warning Larry off his conquest is perfectly territorial and petty.

dialogue.speaker: Arthur
dialogue.line: You stay away from her, shorty.

jokes.text: Marylou, he is planning to put dog poop on vince vaughn's car.
jokes.setup: Mary Lou not understanding the urgency

jokes.explanation: Good callback to the absurd revenge plan. Beverly's matter-of-fact delivery of the insane plan makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Marylou, he is planning to put dog poop on vince vaughn's car.

jokes.text: He blubbered like a fucking baby. No. I mean, it was the funniest goddamn thing you'd ever want to see.

jokes.punchline: His cruel enjoyment of another performer's vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Jimmy's cruel mockery of Tim Allen sets up Larry's upcoming humiliation perfectly. The 'blubbered like a baby' image is vivid.

dialogue.speaker: Jimmy Belushi
dialogue.line: He blubbered like a fucking baby. No. I mean, it was the funniest goddamn thing you'd ever want to see.

jokes.text: What a pussy. Pussy, man.

jokes.punchline: His repeated, emphatic dismissal
jokes.explanation: Jimmy's crude repetition shows his character but isn't particularly clever. Sets up the irony for Larry's upcoming tears.

dialogue.speaker: Jimmy Belushi

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He brought me another cat. And, uh--

jokes.setup: Hank seeing Larry's emotional breakdown
jokes.punchline: His excited repetition about recording it
jokes.explanation: Hank's gleeful repetition shows his excitement at Larry's humiliation. The repetition emphasizes his cruel glee.

jokes.setup: Larry's uncertainty about Hank's painting subject
jokes.punchline: Hank's matter-of-fact confirmation while watching

jokes.punchline: Beverly's perfectly timed confession
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing for the confession - right when Hank is emotionally vulnerable. The simplicity after all the buildup is brilliant.

dialogue.line: I hit your car.

jokes.setup: Vince confronting Hank about his car

jokes.setup: Continuation of Hank's apology letter
jokes.punchline: His casual misogyny followed by movie praise
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - casual sexism followed by sincere movie compliment. 'Hoot and a half' is perfectly dorky Hank language.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Women-- what are you going to do? By the way, I thought swingers was a hoot and a half.

jokes.text: When I broke up with her, she kept leaving dead stuff on my porch for about 6 months. Mainly squirrels.

jokes.punchline: The specific detail about dead squirrels
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'dead stuff' to 'mainly squirrels' is perfect. The specificity makes it both absurd and believable.

dialogue.speaker: Vince
dialogue.line: When I broke up with her, she kept leaving dead stuff on my porch for about 6 months. Mainly squirrels.

jokes.text: They feel closer to you. I-- I can't live like this.
jokes.setup: People complimenting Larry on his emotional interview
jokes.punchline: Larry's horror at genuine human connection
